index_notes: Edit the split files in this directory; do not treat generated bundles as canonical.
Canonical syntax only. Compact syntax is documented separately as a non-normative surface and excluded from the normative source set.
All family and form files use the same normalized structure for syntax patterns, constraints, defaults, derived fields, and encoding sources.
The primary legible reference now lives in docs/src/.

isa.name: OpcodeOne
isa.author: Antonio Rodríguez
notes: This directory is the authoritative machine-readable source for the closed OpcodeOne ISA forms currently captured.
Canonical syntax only. Compact syntax is documented separately as a non-normative surface and remains outside the normative source set.
Numeric family opcode assignments are fixed in opcode_slots.json for the current closed v0.9 families.
Family and form JSON files follow a normalized structure intended to stay stable as the ISA grows.

syntax_model.description: Canonical ISA syntax is expressed as named patterns. Each pattern is an ordered sequence of literal and slot tokens, and named tokens emit the explicit inputs consumed by encoding.

documented_non_normative_surfaces.name: compact
notes: Compact syntax is documented separately from the canonical syntax model.
It may be supported by tooling as an alternate assembly surface, but it is not part of the normative ISA contract.
Compact syntax templates are stored as per-family overlays, and the generated ISA bundle expands them into exhaustive coverage for the current closed canonical patterns.
Compact syntax is an optional assembly surface and does not change ISA semantics.
The normative ISA reference remains canonical syntax only.
The reference assembler currently accepts canonical syntax only.
Tools may expose this surface as sugar, aliases, or future front-end syntax.
The compact overlay files are non-normative source metadata and do not change canonical ISA semantics.
general_rules: Omit helper words such as 'to', 'from', and 'into'.
In TAKE/GIVE byte forms, fold the explicit 'byte' marker into the mnemonic suffix '.B'.
For ALU, logic, and shift/rotate forms, place the mnemonic first.
constraints: The base ISA does not define INCREMENT or DECREMENT, so compact syntax does not define INC or DEC as base instructions.
The base ISA does not define unconditional relative JMP off16; a tool may still offer it as a pseudoinstruction outside this documented base.

examples.canonical: A PLUS B keep and use carry
A MINUS B keep and use borrow
A TIMES B unsigned keep high
A LEFTSHIFT 1 keep and use carry
A RIGHTSHIFT 1 keep and use carry
A logical RIGHTSHIFT 1 keep and use carry

sections.name: jumps
notes: The base ISA does not define a compact unconditional relative jump.

examples.canonical: JUMP -6 when C LESS than D

encoding_model.description: Each form owns one or more machine words. Variable fields read from family opcode, named sequence inputs, or derived fields.

opcode_slots.description: Frozen numeric family opcode slots for the current closed OpcodeOne v0.9 families.

notes: This timing model uses integer picoseconds frozen from the latest 2026-04-23 RP2350 O3+LTO reference VM rerun.
In the current canonical model, 1 chronon equals 1 measured picosecond on the reference VM implementation.
paths contains the canonical exact per-path chronons from the reference run and is the complete timing view.
Instruction forms derive their timing from the exact paths that reference the same family and form.

semantics.summary: Loads one byte from memory at reg24 into the low byte of reg16 and zero-extends the result.
Loads two bytes from memory at reg24 into reg16.
Loads three bytes from memory at reg24 into reg24.
Loads one byte from memory at reg24 plus signed offset into the low byte of reg16 and zero-extends the result.
Loads two bytes from memory at reg24 plus signed offset into reg16.
Loads three bytes from memory at reg24 plus signed offset into reg24.
Stores the low byte of reg16 into memory at reg24.
Stores two bytes from reg16 into memory at reg24.
Stores three bytes from reg24 into memory at reg24.
Stores the low byte of reg16 into memory at reg24 plus signed offset.
Stores two bytes from reg16 into memory at reg24 plus signed offset.
Stores three bytes from reg24 into memory at reg24 plus signed offset.
Loads one byte from absolute memory into the low byte of A, B, C or D and zero-extends the result.
Stores the low byte of A, B, C or D into absolute memory.
Loads two bytes from absolute memory into A, B, C or D.
Stores two bytes from A, B, C or D into absolute memory.
Loads three bytes from absolute memory into reg24.
Stores three bytes from reg24 into absolute memory.
Copies or moves src into dest, allowing reg16/reg24 width conversion by zero-extension or truncation.
Swaps two registers of the same width.
Copies K into a reg24 destination.
Copies a reg24 source into K.
Copies SP into a reg24 destination.
Copies a reg24 source into SP.
Copies PC into a reg24 destination.
Stores reg16 to the ascending stack in little-endian order at the address pointed to by SP, then advances SP by 2 to the next free byte.
Stores reg24 to the ascending stack in little-endian order at the address pointed to by SP, then advances SP by 3 to the next free byte.
Decrements SP by 2 and then reconstructs reg16 from two stack bytes in little-endian order.
Decrements SP by 3 and then reconstructs reg24 from three stack bytes in little-endian order.
Pushes the 24-bit address of the next complete instruction to the ascending stack in little-endian order, then transfers control to an absolute 24-bit destination.
Pushes the 24-bit address of the next complete instruction to the ascending stack in little-endian order, then transfers control to the 24-bit destination held in reg24.
Decrements SP by 3, reconstructs a 24-bit return address from stack bytes in little-endian order, and transfers control to it.
Decrements SP by 3, reconstructs a 24-bit return address from stack bytes in little-endian order, enables global maskable interrupts, and transfers control to it.
No operation. Execution continues at the next instruction.
Enables maskable interruptions. Delivery and handler dispatch remain platform-defined.
Stops execution. Post-stop behavior depends on the execution environment.
Disables maskable interruptions. Non-maskable events remain outside the base ISA contract.
Adds src to dest. Optional carry-in/carry-out behavior is controlled by the modifier and K.
Adds a signed 6-bit immediate to dest.
Adds a signed 16-bit immediate to dest.
Subtracts src from dest. Optional borrow-in/borrow-out behavior is controlled by the modifier and K.
Subtracts a signed 6-bit immediate from dest.
Subtracts a signed 16-bit immediate from dest.
Multiplies src into dest, with optional unsigned mode and optional keep high in K for 16-bit forms.
Divides or computes modulus of dest by src, signed by default and optionally unsigned. In signed mode, division truncates toward zero and MODULE keeps the sign of the dividend in the remainder.
Applies unary arithmetic negation or absolute value to dest.
Loads an unsigned 6-bit immediate into dest, zero-extending when dest is reg24.
Loads a raw 16-bit immediate into dest, zero-extending when dest is reg24.
Performs AND, OR, XOR, or unary NOT.
Performs AND, OR, or XOR with an immediate.
Tests, sets, clears or toggles a single bit.
Writes the min or max of src and dest back to dest, signed or unsigned. If both operands compare equal, dest keeps that same value with no additional side effects.

Shift by a register count, optionally storing the last shifted-out bit in K. With reg_count, count=0 leaves dest unchanged and does not update K; LEFTSHIFT and logical RIGHTSHIFT with count>width yield 0, while arithmetic RIGHTSHIFT with count>=width saturates to the original sign bit.
Single-bit through-carry shift using K as input, optionally writing the shifted-out bit back to K. The effective count is always 1.
Pure left or right rotation. K is not used. The effective count is reduced modulo the operand width, so count=0 and count=width preserve the original value.
Unconditional absolute jump.
Unconditional jump to the 24-bit address stored in a reg24 operand.
Conditional relative jump using EQUAL, LESS than, BELOW, or CHECK. The signed off16 is measured in bytes from the next complete instruction; CHECK means (lhs & rhs) != 0.
